content.extracted_text:
Vat et
i,
q 45. [Fine Bindings] STRACHEY, ELIZABETH. Translations from the MES
French of Emile Souvestre. N.p. (Privately Printed), 1856.
$1250.00
Two vols., sm. 4to., full dark blue ctushed-morocco over
covers heavily ornamented with gilt and blind relief decoration, far too elaborate to
describe. At the top and bottom (on the edges of the outer gilt fillet borders) are
two gilt lines: TOQ*THE*MEMORY*OF*ELIZABETH STRACHEY* and « NON «
ANIMUM:. Board edges gilt, inner gilt dentelles, a.e.g. On the inside rear paste-down
of the first volume is the binder’s ticket of Leighton Son & On the front fly
leaf of volume one is the inscription, “Constance Strachey from E. S. 1 June 1887.”
Elizabeth Strachey was born 14 February. 1810, the eldest daughter of R. W.
| Wilkinson of Hall, Bedfordshire. She married Edward Strachey of
Sutton Court, Pensford, Somerset on 27 August 1844 and died without issue on 11] et
ASE April 1855. Emile Souvestre (1806-1854) was a French playwright who did
much better with his novels than for the stage, although he deliberate y aimed at
making the novel an engine of moral instruction. He is said to have harmo- a hs
ny with Mrs. Strachey’s translations of his works, which include The Bargeman of
the Loire, The Lazaretto-Keeper, Confessions of a Working Man, The Sea-Shore
Gleaner, An Attic Philosopher in Paris, Family Memoirs and The Widower’s Old ON
Age (three chapters of which Edward Strachey added to Vol. IT from M. Souvestre’s
unfinished work). These volumes were published: “Jo the memory of her to whom
these translations are chiefly due, while her hand, in them, as in all t ings else, was ever
joined with that of her husband.”
vA The bindings by Leighton could not be more elaborate and were certainly done
expressly for these privately printed volumes. We know of one other copy of the
work in identical bindings in a private London collection (there are no copies in
either The British or NUC). The brass stamping plates were used again by
| Leighton for an 1868 edition of Oliver Goldsmith's The Traveler in a somewhat
j altered fashion (i.e. he removed the memorial lines at the top and bottom, replac- a
ing two lines of gilt decoration to try and achieve balance; he also ORE
4 removed Elizabeth Strachey’s initials from the central panel on the covers, replacing -
it with an uninspired design element). These beautiful and rare bindings are in FINE A
| CONDITION,

audit_trail.name: Greyscale Conversion
audit_trail.description: Converted BGR input to single-channel greyscale. Colour is irrelevant for ink-on-paper OCR and removing it reduces processing cost by 3×.

audit_trail.name: Bilateral Denoising Filter
audit_trail.description: Applied edge-aware bilateral filter (d=9, σColor=75, σSpace=75). Suppresses paper grain and scanner noise while preserving the sharp ink-stroke boundaries that OCR depends on.

audit_trail.name: CLAHE Contrast Enhancement
audit_trail.description: Applied Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (clipLimit=2.0, tile=8×8) to recover faded ink strokes and compensate for lighting gradients across the document surface.

audit_trail.name: Adaptive Gaussian Binarization
audit_trail.description: Converted to binary using local Gaussian-weighted thresholding (blockSize=15, C=10). Each pixel is compared to its 15×15 local neighbourhood average, isolating ink strokes from yellow staining and uneven lighting that would defeat global thresholding.

audit_trail.name: OCR — Tesseract LSTM (PSM 6, OEM 3)
audit_trail.description: Recognised text at 72% mean confidence. 67% of tokens high-confidence (≥80%). Tokens below 40% confidence dropped from extracted_text to eliminate scanner-border noise.

audit_trail.name: Metadata Extraction
audit_trail.description: Regex entity extraction found 10 date(s), 17 person(s), 2 place(s). Document type: Book Catalogue Entry.

audit_trail.name: Annotated Image
audit_trail.description: Drew per-word bounding boxes colour-coded green/yellow/red by OCR confidence tier (≥80 / 50-79 / <50).

audit_trail.name: Layout Map
audit_trail.description: Morphological dilation (kernel 15×5, 2 iterations) merged characters into paragraph blobs; contours mark layout regions.

audit_trail.name: Table Detection
audit_trail.description: Morphological line extraction + numeric-alignment heuristic. Found 1 table(s). Note: borderless tables require deep-learning models (future work).

ai_insights:
*   Role: Document analysis assistant for anthropology and social science students.
*   Input: OCR-extracted text from a historical document.
*   Output Requirements:
    1.  Document Type (1-2 sentences).
    2.  Estimated Era/Period (1-2 sentences).
    3.  Key Entities (1-2 sentences).
    4.  Historical Context (1-2 sentences).
    5.  Research Value (1-2 sentences).
*   Constraint: Be factual based *only* on the text. Keep sections brief.

    *   *Content:* Describes a set of books: "Translations from the MES French of Emile Souvestre" by Elizabeth Strachey. Mentions price ($1250.00), binding details (Leighton Son & Co.), inscriptions ("Constance Strachey from E. S. 1 June 1887"), biographical info on Elizabeth Strachey and Emile Souvestre, and rarity/condition details.
    *   *Format:* It looks like a listing for a rare book sale or an auction catalog entry. It includes a price, physical description, provenance, and rarity notes.

    *   *Document Type:* It is a catalog entry or a sales listing for a rare book/fine binding.
    *   *Estimated Era/Period:* The text mentions dates like 1856 (publication), 1887 (inscription), and 1868 (other edition). The document itself seems modern (due to the price format "$1250.00" and the descriptive style), but it describes items from the mid-to-late 19th century. *Correction:* The prompt asks for the era of the *document*. The document is a modern antiquarian book catalog entry describing 19th-century items.
    *   *Key Entities:* Elizabeth Strachey, Emile Souvestre, Edward Strachey, R. W. Wilkinson, Constance Strachey, Leighton Son & Co. (binder), Sutton Court (Somerset), Hall (Bedfordshire).
    *   *Historical Context:* The text touches on 19th-century literary translation, the role of women in publishing/translation, and the social practice of private printing and elaborate bookbinding as status symbols.
    *   *Research Value:* A student could use this to study 19th-century gender roles in literature, the history of bookbinding/material culture, or the social networks of the Victorian-era upper class through provenance and inscriptions.

    *   *Document Type:* This is a catalog entry or sales listing for a rare book and fine binding.
    *   *Estimated Era/Period:* While describing 19th-century items, the document itself is a modern antiquarian book catalog.
    *   *Key Entities:* Key figures include Elizabeth Strachey, Emile Souvestre, Edward Strachey, and the binder Leighton Son & Co.
    *   *Historical Context:* The text reflects 19th-century literary culture, specifically regarding female translators and the practice of private printing for personal or memorial purposes.
    *   *Research Value:* Students could use this to research material culture (bookbinding), the history of women in the literary sphere, or social history through personal inscriptions and provenance.

    *   1-2 sentences per section? Yes.
    *   Factual based only on text? Yes.
    *   Anthropology/Social Science focus? Yes.
**1. Document Type**
This is a catalog entry or sales listing for a rare book and fine binding. It provides a detailed physical description, provenance, and pricing for a specific set of volumes.

**2. Estimated Era/Period**
The document is a modern antiquarian book catalog describing items and biographical events from the mid-to-late 19th century.

**3. Key Entities**
Key individuals mentioned include Elizabeth Strachey (translator), Emile Souvestre (French playwright), Edward Strachey, and the binder Leighton Son & Co. Notable locations include Hall, Bedfordshire, and Sutton Court, Somerset.

**4. Historical Context**
The text reflects 19th-century literary and social practices, such as the role of women in translation, the use of private printing for memorial purposes, and the high value placed on elaborate bookbinding as a status symbol.

**5. Research Value**
A student could use this document to study material culture (specifically Victorian bookbinding), the history of women's contributions to literature, or social history through the study of personal inscriptions and provenance.
